title: RAG Explained in 100 Seconds — How AI Stops Hallucinating - Devoctal (360p).mp4
transcript: AI lies to you, and it's not even its fault. Every LLM, GPT, Claude, Gemini, is trained once on a fixed snapshot of the world. After that, it's frozen. No live data, no access to your documents, no memory of what happened yesterday. So when it doesn't know something, it doesn't say, I don't know. It generates a confident-sounding best guess. That's called a hallucination. And it's not a bug. It's how the architecture works by default. RAG was built to fix exactly that. RAG, Retrieval Augmented Generation, is the architecture that makes AI actually look things up before answering, not guess. Here's exactly how it works. One, your data, PDFs, wikis, emails, databases, is ingested and split into chunks. 2. Each chunk is converted into an embedding, a vector of numbers that encodes meaning, and stored in a vector database. 3. Your question comes in and gets embedded the same way. 4. The system finds the chunks whose vectors are closest to your question, meaning beats keywords. 5. Those chunks get injected into the AI's prompt, and it answers grounded only in what it just retrieved. Think of it as an open book exam. A standard LLM answers from memory. RAG checks the book, finds the right page, then answers. RAG doesn't make AI smarter. It makes AI honest. The best model isn't the one that knows everything. It's the one that knows where to look. RAG is just one piece. If you want to understand what's really happening inside AI tools, watch this next. And if these breakdowns help, subscribe. I'll see you there.
